coredata.dc:title: Preparation of PEDOT:PSS thin films doped with graphene and graphene quantum dots

Marković, Zoran M.
Jovanović, Svetlana P.
Peruško, Davor B.
Budimir, Milica D.
Holclajtner-Antunović, Ivanka D.
Pavlović, Vladimir B.
Todorović Marković, Biljana M.
coredata.dc:description: Enhanced conductivity, transparency and stability are the most important factors to consider in order to prepare electrodes for optoelectronic devices. In this paper, we investigated the properties of poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) films doped with graphene and graphene quantum dots. Samples were deposited by spin-coating onto glass substrates. Thin films were characterized by UV–vis and Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and four-point probe measurements. It was found that both graphene and graphene quantum dots improve the conductivity of PEDOT:PSS films with only small decrease in transparency. AFM and SEM analysis showed homogenous distribution of spherical nanoparticles of graphene quantum dots and irregular shaped nanoparticles of graphene in PEDOT:PSS.

Thin films
Graphene
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of graphene–graphene quantum dots colloid
Preparation PEDOT:PSS–GQD and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD thin films
Morphology and composition of GQDs and gGQDs
Morphology and composition of PEDOT:PSS–GQD and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD thin films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM images of (a) GQDs and (b) gGQDs with the corresponding TEM images in the inset.

UV–vis spectra of GQDs (black line) and gGQDs (red line). Upper right corner represents the images of both colloids. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Raman spectrum of gGQDs and 2D-peak fitted with four Lorentz functions (upper right corner). Raman spectrum is normalized to G-peak.
AFM images of (a) pristine PEDOT:PSS, (b) PEDOT:PSS–GQD, (c) PEDOT:PSS–gGQD, and (d) SEM image of PEDOT:PSS–GQD.

Raman spectra of pristine PEDOT:PSS (black line), PEDOT:PSS–GQDs (red line), and PEDOT:PSS–gGQDs (blue line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fitted Raman spectra of PEDOT:PSS, PEDOT:PSS-GQD, and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD, respectively.

Transmission spectra of PEDOT:PSS (black line), PEDOT:PSS–GQD (red line), and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD (blue line) samples. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Sheet resistance (R s) of samples PEDOT:PSS, PEDOT:PSS–GQD, and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD.

head.ce:title: Preparation of PEDOT:PSS thin films doped with graphene and graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences, University of Belgrade, Mike Alasa 12-14, Belgrade 11001, Serbia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Physical Chemistry, University of Belgrade, Studentski trg 12-16, Belgrade 11000, Serbia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Agriculture, Department of Agricultural Engineering, University of Belgrade, Nemanjina 6, Zemun 11080, Serbia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enhanced conductivity, transparency and stability are the most important factors to consider in order to prepare electrodes for optoelectronic devices. In this paper, we investigated the properties of poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) films doped with graphene and graphene quantum dots. Samples were deposited by spin-coating onto glass substrates. Thin films were characterized by UV–vis and Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and four-point probe measurements. It was found that both graphene and graphene quantum dots improve the conductivity of PEDOT:PSS films with only small decrease in transparency. AFM and SEM analysis showed homogenous distribution of spherical nanoparticles of graphene quantum dots and irregular shaped nanoparticles of graphene in PEDOT:PSS.

ce:section.ce:para: Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) is organic conducting polymer that can be used as the active material in flexible organic electronics due to its remarkably high conductivity, transparency and environmental stability [1]. Poor solubility of PEDOT is overcome by adding a water-soluble polyelectrolyte poly(styrenesulfonic acid) (PSS) as a charge balancing dopant to increase the stability of PEDOT:PSS colloidal solution in water for easily processing by spin-coating or inkjet printing techniques. However, electrical conductivity of PEDOT:PSS complex is not high enough for high performance applications which results in electronics packaging with poor efficiency and reliability. Therefore, several research groups attempted to enhance the electrical conductivity of PEDOT:PSS by secondary solvent doping [2] and incorporating nanoparticles such as carbon nanotubes [3–5] and graphene [6–8].

Graphene is the material that has been attracted enormous scientific attention due to its unique electronic and mechanical properties, which makes it suitable to be used in flexible transparent electronics [9,10]. Carbon atoms in graphene are sp2 hybridized and covalently bonded to form a honeycomb structure. One of the most commonly used procedures to obtain graphene is Hummer’s method which involves oxidation of graphite with strong oxidative acids to graphene oxide followed by its reduction to graphene [11]. This method has several disadvantages, including poor electrical conductivity of graphene stemming from defected structure, its irreversible agglomeration, and the method itself is time consuming. One way to overcome these problems is the exfoliation of graphite under ultrasound using surface active agents, such as sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate (SDBS) [12], sodium dodecylsulfonate (SDS) [13], polyvinyl pirolidone (PVP) [14] and polyethylene glycol (PEG) [15] among others. Recently, it was found that graphene quantum dots (GQDs) show excellent potential to prepare stable water dispersion of graphene [16]. Due to remarkable water solubility of GQDs conditioned by the presence of oxygen containing functional groups and the ability to form π–π interactions with graphene sheets, it is possible to obtain graphene dispersion that possess long term stability.

In this paper, we prepared thin films of mixtures of PEDOT:PSS and graphene quantum dots as well as PEDOT:PSS and graphene colloid stabilized with graphene quantum dots. Those mixtures were deposited onto glass substrates by spin-coating and the conductivity and optical transparency have been measured and compared with as-deposited PEDOT:PSS film. Morphology of the samples was investigated using atomic force microscopy, transmission electron microscopy and scanning electron microscopy.

A stable ethanol dispersion of GQDs was prepared using graphite rods as both anode and cathode and NaOH/ethanol as electrolyte, with the current intensity set to 20mA [17,18]. After evaporation of ethanol, GQDs were dilluted in Millipore distilled/deionized water. Water dispersion of GQDs was filtered using column chromatography with cationic Dowex 50W X8 resin (Carl Roth GmbH). GQDs were further dried at 100°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of graphene–graphene quantum dots colloid
ce:section.ce:para: An amount of 100mg of graphite powder (Timrex KS6, Timcal Ltd.) was added into 30ml of GQD dispersion (0.1mg/ml) and it was sonicated (ultrasonic bath power 750W) for 24h at room temperature. After sonication, GQDs–graphene dispersion was centrifuged at 2000× g for 1h to precipitate large graphite particles. Supernatant (further, in the text referred as gGQDs) was used for further procedure. gGQDs were further dried at 100°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation PEDOT:PSS–GQD and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD thin films
ce:section.ce:para: To make PEDOT:PSS–GQD thin films, in 1ml PEDOT:PSS dispersion (1.3wt%, Sigma–Aldrich) was added an amount of GQDs to obtain concentration of 10, 5, 2, 1 and 0.5mg/ml GQDs in PEDOT:PSS and sonicated for 30min. After sonication dispersions were spin-coated onto glass substrates (5000rpm, 1min) and left in air to dry. For the GQDs concentration in PEDOT:PSS of 10mg/ml and 5mg/ml, films are very inhomogeneous with lots of large dots on the surface originating from undispersed GQDs. GQDs in these concentrations remain undispersed even after prolonged period of ultrasonication, therefore those samples will not be considered.

PEDOT:PSS–gGQD films were prepared by mixing 2, 1 and 0.5mg of dried gGQDs and 1ml PEDOT:PSS dispersion in order to obtain concentrations of gGQDs in polymer of 2, 1 and 0.5mg/ml, respectively. All samples were sonicated using ultrasound bath for 30min and deposited by spin-coating under the same conditions. For comparison, PEDOT:PSS was spin-coated onto glass substrates under the same conditions.

UV–vis measurements were conducted using Avantes UV–vis spectrophotometer. All samples were scanned at room temperature. For absorbance measurements both GQD and gGQD colloid were properly diluted. Absorbance spectra were automatically corrected to suspending medium (water) and the transmittance spectra were automatically corrected to substrate (glass). The transmittance spectra of all thin film samples were recorded in the range from 250 to 900nm.

Raman spectra were obtained by DXR Raman microscope (Thermo Scientific) using 532nm excitation line with power of 5mW. The spectral resolution was 1cm−1. Acquisition time was 10×10s. Raman spectra were recorded at room temperature.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were performed using Quesant microscope operating in tapping mode in air at room temperature. Standard silicon tips (purchased from Nano and More GmbH) with constant force of 40N/m were used. Lateral sizes and heights were estimated using Gwyddion software.
Transmission electronic microscopy (TEM) images were obtained by Philips EM 208 microscope. The samples were prepared by casting a drop of the gGQDs dispersion onto carbon coated Cu grids.

The morphology of the films was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-6390LV). The samples for SEM analysis were coated with gold using the physical vapor deposition (PVD) process. Samples were covered with gold (Baltec SCD 005 Sputter Coater) using 30mA current from the distance of 50mm during 180s. Conductivity of samples was measured by commercial four-point probe method at ambient temperature under normal laboratory conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and composition of GQDs and gGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Size distribution and morphology of GQDs and gGQDs nanoparticles was investigated by AFM and TEM and the results are presented in Fig. 1 . As it can be seen from Fig. 1a, GQDs have sphere-like structures (TEM micrograph) and tend to agglomerate upon drying (top view AFM image). Lateral size of GQDs lies in the range of ∼20–60nm, with the average value of 35nm. As for height of GQDs all samples exhibit a height distribution within ∼1–10nm, implying that most of synthesized GQDs composed of few-layer graphene sheets (1–10nm). In Fig. 1b top view AFM image and TEM micrograph of graphene flakes are shown. Their average height is 6nm.

Fig. 2 represents UV–vis spectra of diluted GQD and gGQD colloid. In the case of graphene, the π–π* transition peak is at 270nm with the disappearance of n–π* transition peak [19]. These peaks can also be observed in the UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs, with the π–π* transition peak centered at a wavelength between 200 and 270nm and the n–π* transition peak at wavelength longer than 260nm [20,21]. Both UV–vis spectra of GQDs and gGQDs (Fig. 2) are very similar, which indicates the presence of the same chromophores in both samples. In UV–vis spectra one can observe a shoulder at about 260nm, which is more prominent in GQDs spectrum (black line). This shoulder originates from π → π * transition of CC bond and is blue shifted due to the effect of polar solvent.

Fig. 3 shows the Raman spectrum of gGQD sample in which three well defined narrow peaks can be clearly seen. The peak with maximum intensity at 1573cm−1 (G-peak) is attributed to the vibration of sp2 bonded carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice. Peak at 1342cm−1 (D-peak) is associated with vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in plane terminations of the disordered graphite and related to the defects and disorders in structures in carbon materials. Peak at 2680cm−1 (2D-peak) is the second order of the D-peak and has approximately the same height as D-peak. The intensity ratio for the D-band and G-band (I D/I G ratio) is widely used for characterizing the defect quantity in graphitic materials [22]. Gupta et al. showed that for one-layer graphene D-band intensity (I D) is ∼1/5 of that of the G-band (I G) and I D/I G ratio exponentially declines with the increase of number of graphene layers [23]. For gGQDs sample, I D/I G ratio is 0.38. This high value of I D/I G ratio is the cumulative consequence of dangling bonds of graphene planes and disorder in the structure of GQDs.

Raman spectroscopy can clearly distinguish a single and a few layer graphene from bulk graphite according to shape, intensity, and frequency of 2D-peak [24–27]. Ferrari et al. showed that 2D-peak in bulk graphite consists of two components 2D 1 and 2D 2, while bilayer graphene consists of four 2D components: 2D 1B, 2D 1A, 2D 2A, 2D 2B [28]. 2D-peaks of gGQDs sample can be fitted with four Lorentz functions, indicating that gGQDs consist of a bilayer graphene (inset of Fig. 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and composition of PEDOT:PSS–GQD and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD thin films
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of thin films was investigated using the AFM and SEM (Fig. 4 ). The AFM image shown in Fig. 4a suggests that the surface of pristine PEDOT:PSS film is very rough with grain-like structures, which is in the accordance with literature data [29]. In Fig. 4b spherical structures of PEDOT:PSS–GQDs are clearly visible. Polymer wrapping around GQDs causes an increase in size of PEDOT:PSS–GQDs nanoparticles. The diameter of those structures are in the range from 106 to 172nm with the average diameter of 134nm and they are stacked onto polymer chains of PEDOT:PSS (Fig. 4d). PEDOT:PSS–gGQD film (Fig. 4c) consists of graphene nanoparticles of irregular shape homogenously distributed in PEDOT:PSS.

Raman spectra of samples PEDOT:PSS, PEDOT:PSS–GQD (2mg/ml) and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD (2mg/ml) are presented in Fig. 5 . All spectra have the most intense peak at 1436cm−1 which originates from CαCβ symmetric vibration for the five members tiophene ring on the PEDOT chains. Beside, in all Raman spectra there are peaks at 445cm−1 (SO2 bending), 575cm−1 (oxyetilene ring deformation), 989cm−1 (CC alkyl stretching), 1102cm−1 (CαCα stretching), 1266cm−1 (CαCα inter-ring stretching), 1364cm−1 (CβCβ stretching), 1504cm−1 (Cα′ Cβ′ stretching), and 1558cm−1 (quinoid structure) [30–32]. In spectra of PEDOT:PSS–gGQD it is noticeable the absence of both D- and G-peaks at ∼1340cm−1 and ∼1580cm−1, respectively. This might be due to the structure of PEDOT:PSS–gGQD: polymer chains and graphene form a tightly coated layer on the graphene surface due to strong π–π interactions. Kim et al. have found that increasing of graphene content in PEDOT:PSS leads to the decrease of peak intensities that belong to PEDOT:PSS chains, while D- and G-bands of graphene become more dominant [6]. However, when the graphene content in the mixture is lower than 1wt%, D- and G-bands are strongly overlapped by PEDOT:PSS peaks.

Fig. 6 represents Raman spectra in the region from 1300 to 1600cm−1 of PEDOT:PSS, PEDOT:PSS–GQD (2mg/ml), and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD (2mg/ml), respectively. Spectra of pristine PEDOT:PSS were fitted with combination of 8 Lorentz functions, while spectra of doped PEDOT:PSS were fitted with combination of 9 Lorentz functions.

The largest change among Raman spectra of pristine and doped PEDOT:PSS is shoulder at the peak at 1436cm−1. Spectra of doped PEDOT:PSS contain extra Lorentz function at this region. Symmetric CαCβ stretching vibration consists of two components – from the neutral and oxidized structure of polymer. Neutral component at 1433.91cm−1 in pristine PEDOT:PSS shifts to 1435.68 (for sample PEDOT:PSS–GQD) and 1435.98cm−1 (for sample PEDOT:PSS–gGQD). For doped PEDOT:PSS, vibration from neutral structure shifts to higher wavenumbers and vibration of the oxidized structure becomes more intense [33]. Vibration of oxidized structure in pristine PEDOT:PSS at 1449cm−1 is replaced with two vibrations. Former vibration was at 1449cm−1 while later vibration of significant intensity was at 1455.44 (PEDOT:PSS–GQD) and 1454.57cm−1 (PEDOT:PSS–gGQD). This behavior is very similar to that previously reported for chemically doped PEDOT powder, in which the apparent positions of the band due to symmetric CαCβ stretching band in the Raman spectrum is also dependent on the proportion of the oxidized and neutral forms of polymer [34]. Intensity of Lorentz function with maximum at 1539.23cm−1 increased two times in spectra of doped PEDOT:PSS films.

Transmission spectra of PEDOT:PSS, PEDOT:PSS–GQD (2mg/ml), and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD (2mg/ml) samples are shown in Fig. 7 . The transmittance of pristine PEDOT:PSS film at 550nm is 96% and does not decrease throughout the visible range. Transmittance of PEDOT:PSS–GQD as well as PEDOT:PSS–gGQD film is lower and has the value of 87% and 86%, respectively. PEDOT:PSS–GQD and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD samples also do not show any decrease in transparency in the aforementioned region. High transparency level of PEDOT:PSS–gGQD film is in accordance with literature data [35,36]. Due to the high transparency, PEDOT:PSS–GQD and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD films are good candidates for transparent electrodes in optoelectronic devices.

Four-point probe was used to measure the sheet resistance (R s) of each sample, using the formula R s =4.532(U/I). Measurements were conducted at 10 different locations on each film and average values were taken. The results are presented in Table 1 . As it can be seen from the table above, the highest sheet resistance of 480.4kΩ/cm2 has PEDOT:PSS film. Sheet resistance of PEDOT:PSS–GQD decreases with the increase of GQDs concentration, reaching the minimum value of 383.4kΩ/cm2, which is about 20% lower than sheet resistance of PEDOT:PSS. PEDOT:PSS–gGQD sample has the same trend of sheet resistance decreasing, and for gGQDs concentration of 2mg/ml sheet resistance is more than 50% lower than the sheet resistance of PEDOT:PSS. Lowering of sheet resistance of PEDOT:PSS films doped with graphene-like materials has been previously reported. Chang et al. used graphene stabilized with sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS) to obtain graphene doped PEDOT:PSS films with sheet resistance of only 80Ω/cm2 and transmittance of 79% at 550nm [37]. Jo et al. prepared PEDOT:PSS films doped with reduced graphene oxide with sheet resistance of 2.3kΩ/cm2 and transmittance of 80% at 550nm [38]. Wu et al. used graphene oxide stabilized with SDBS to prepare films of doped PEDOT:PSS that have sheet resistance of 85Ω/cm2 and transmittance of 87% at 550nm [39]. Increasing in conductivity of PEDOT:PSS–GQD and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD samples could be explained by the formation of more conductive pathways in PEDOT:PSS matrice upon doping with GQDs and gGQDs.

In this paper, we investigated the possibility to obtain stable graphene colloid using graphene quantum dots as surface active agents and preparation of PEDOT:PSS thin films doped with graphene and graphene quantum dots. AFM and SEM measurements showed homogenous distribution of both graphene and graphene quantum dots nanoparticles in PEDOT:PSS. Both PEDOT:PSS–GQD and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD thin films have high transmittance value of 87 and 86%, respectively. Raman spectroscopy showed that PEDOT:PSS chains form π–π interactions with graphene and graphene quantum dots nanoparticles. Sheet resistance of PEDOT:PSS–GQD as well as PEDOT:PSS–gGQD is lower than sheet resistance of PEDOT:PSS sample. Increased conductivity and high transparency makes PEDOT:PSS–GQD and PEDOT:PSS–gGQD good candidates for preparation of electrodes for optoelectronic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of Republic of Serbia (project no. 172003).
